annotations.text: The accumulation of porphyrin precursors (delta-aminolevulinic acid [ALA] or porphobilinogen [PBG]) or porphyrins (i.e., uroporphyrin, coproporphyrin, or protoporphyrin) leads to the clinical features of porphyria.
Accumulation of the porphyrin precursors causes damage to neurons in the gut, brain, and peripheral nervous system; accumulation of porphyrins causes photosensitivity and damage to skin.
The specific enzyme deficiency and the corresponding accumulation of precursors lead to the differing patterns of disease in porphyria.
Porphyria can be categorized as either acute hepatic (neurovisceral) or cutaneous.
Lead poisoning causes a range of nonspecific symptoms, depending on the blood lead level.

More severe symptoms are seen with high lead levels (>70 μg per deciliter) and can include anemia, constipation, colicky abdominal pain, and peripheral neuropathy; if the level is higher than 100 μg per deciliter, cerebral edema, encephalopathy, coma, or death can occur.

An elevation in the ALA level but not the PBG level, as was the case in this patient, suggests ALA dehydratase deficiency, which is associated with ALA dehydratase–deficiency porphyria (an extremely rare disease), lead poisoning, and hereditary tyrosinemia.
ALA dehydratase inhibition can fully explain the elevations in the levels of urinary ALA, coproporphyrin, and erythrocyte zinc protoporphyrin that are seen in persons with lead poisoning.
This patient’s blood lead level was markedly increased, at 104 μg per deciliter (reference value, <4.9), which confirmed a diagnosis of lead poisoning
Lead poisoning appeared to be the major contributor to her anemia; the hemoglobin level increased as the blood lead levels declined.
Lead poisoning can result in permanent effects on cognition, even in adults.

The contribution of lead to this patient’s cognitive problems is difficult to determine with certainty, given her initial presentation with severe hyponatremia and emotional distress and later, the presence of stress, anxiety, and depression.
